prompt:
A consumer group compared the longevity of two types of razor blades: an inexpensive generic blade and a more expensive name-brand blade. Two independent samples were randomly selected, one consisted of ten generic blades and the other consisted of ten name-brand blades. The number of shaves obtained before a blade became too dull to provide a close shave was recorded for each blade in the two independent samples.

| Number of Close Shaves for the Generic (G) Type | 99 | 111 | 97 | 101 | 118 | 108 | 96 | 129 | 80 | 136 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|

| Number of Close Shaves for the Name-brand (B) Type | 112 | 105 | 109 | 122 | 130 | 113 | 106 | 116 | 103 | 110 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|

(a) Create a back-to-back stemplot to display the distributions of the data.

(b) In a few sentences, compare the distributions in part (a).

(c) To test whether the name-brand blades tend to last longer than the generic blades on average, a two-sample t-test was performed. The conditions for inference were checked and verified. The test statistic was $t=0.87$, with a p-value of 0.20. What should be concluded about whether the name-brand blades last significantly longer than the generic blades? Explain.

Also of interest to the consumer group is whether the generic (G) blades have more variability in the number of close shaves than the name-brand (B) blades. The consumer group intends to use the ratio of the sample standard deviations, $s_G/s_B$, as a test statistic to test the hypotheses $H_0:\sigma_G=\sigma_B$ versus $H_a:\sigma_G>\sigma_B$.

(d) Explain why the median of the distribution of a sample of 1,000 of these simulated ratios will be about 1.

(e) A histogram for the 1,000 simulated values of the ratio $s_G/s_B$ is shown below. Summary statistics for the original data are provided in the table below.

| Type | Sample Size | Sample Mean | Sample Standard Deviation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Generic (G) | 10 | 107.5 | 16.70 |
| Name Brand (B) | 10 | 112.6 | 8.28 |

Is there convincing evidence that the generic blades have more variability in the number of close shaves than the name-brand blades? Explain.

points.criteria: Question 6 Intent of Question The primary goals of this question were to assess a student's ability to understand, apply, and draw conclusions from a simulation analysis beyond what was previously studied. More specific goals are to assess a student's ability to (1) produce a graphical display and use it to compare distributions between two groups; (2) draw an appropriate conclusion from a test statistic and a p-value; and (3) understand and interpret simulation results to draw a conclusion about a hypothesis testing scenario not previously studied. Solution Part (a): The back-to-back stemplot is as follows. Number of shaves Leaf unit = 1.0 0|8 represents 80 shaves. Generic (G) Name Brand (B) 0 8 9 7 6 9 8 1 10 3 5 6 9 8 1 11 0 2 3 6 9 12 2 6 13 0 Part (b): The name brand blades generally produce more shaves than the generic blades; the median number of shaves for the name brand blades is 111.5, which is higher than the median number of shaves for the generic blades of 104.5. The generic blades display more variability in number of shaves than the name brand blades. Both distributions of number of shaves are slightly skewed to the right, and no outliers are apparent in either direction. Part (c): The p-value of 0.20 is greater than the conventional significance levels of 0.10 $\alpha$ = or 0.05 $\alpha$ = . Therefore the sample data do not provide enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis that there is no difference in the mean number of shaves produced by the generic and the name brand blades. So we cannot conclude that the name brand blades last significantly longer than the generic blades, on average. Question 6 (continued) Part (d): The simulation was conducted under the assumption that the population standard deviations are equal (so, / 1 G B $\sigma$ $\sigma$ = ) and the parent population from which the samples were drawn is normal. Therefore we expect that with a very large number of random samples, the sample standard deviation for the name brand group will be greater than the sample standard deviation for the generic group about half of the time and will be less about half of the time. As a consequence, the ratio of the two sample standard deviations should be less than 1 about half of the time and greater than 1 about half of the time. Because the median of a distribution is the value that is exceeded half of the time and not exceeded half of the time, we expect the median of these ratios to equal 1. Part (e): Yes, there is evidence that the generic blades (G) have more variability than the name brand blades (B). The ratio of sample standard deviations for the observed sample data is 16.70 2.017 8.28 $\approx$ . The simulation was conducted assuming that the population standard deviations are equal. The value of 2.017 appears fairly far in the right tail of the distribution of simulated ratios. In fact, it appears that less than 5% of the simulated ratios are as large, or larger than, the observed ratio of 2.017. Because the observed ratio is unlikely to occur by random sampling alone when the population standard deviations are equal, we have fairly strong evidence that the generic blades (G) really do have more variability in the number of shaves than the name brand blades (B). rubric_text: Question 6 Intent of Question The primary goals of this question were to assess a student's ability to understand, apply, and draw conclusions from a simulation analysis beyond what was previously studied. More specific goals are to assess a student's ability to (1) produce a graphical display and use it to compare distributions between two groups; (2) draw an appropriate conclusion from a test statistic and a p-value; and (3) understand and interpret simulation results to draw a conclusion about a hypothesis testing scenario not previously studied. Solution Part (a): The back-to-back stemplot is as follows. Number of shaves Leaf unit = 1.0 0|8 represents 80 shaves. Generic (G) Name Brand (B) 0 8 9 7 6 9 8 1 10 3 5 6 9 8 1 11 0 2 3 6 9 12 2 6 13 0 Part (b): The name brand blades generally produce more shaves than the generic blades; the median number of shaves for the name brand blades is 111.5, which is higher than the median number of shaves for the generic blades of 104.5. The generic blades display more variability in number of shaves than the name brand blades. Both distributions of number of shaves are slightly skewed to the right, and no outliers are apparent in either direction. Part (c): The p-value of 0.20 is greater than the conventional significance levels of 0.10 $\alpha$ = or 0.05 $\alpha$ = . Therefore the sample data do not provide enough evidence to reject the null hypothesis that there is no difference in the mean number of shaves produced by the generic and the name brand blades. So we cannot conclude that the name brand blades last significantly longer than the generic blades, on average. Question 6 (continued) Part (d): The simulation was conducted under the assumption that the population standard deviations are equal (so, / 1 G B $\sigma$ $\sigma$ = ) and the parent population from which the samples were drawn is normal. Therefore we expect that with a very large number of random samples, the sample standard deviation for the name brand group will be greater than the sample standard deviation for the generic group about half of the time and will be less about half of the time. As a consequence, the ratio of the two sample standard deviations should be less than 1 about half of the time and greater than 1 about half of the time. Because the median of a distribution is the value that is exceeded half of the time and not exceeded half of the time, we expect the median of these ratios to equal 1. Part (e): Yes, there is evidence that the generic blades (G) have more variability than the name brand blades (B). The ratio of sample standard deviations for the observed sample data is 16.70 2.017 8.28 $\approx$ . The simulation was conducted assuming that the population standard deviations are equal. The value of 2.017 appears fairly far in the right tail of the distribution of simulated ratios. In fact, it appears that less than 5% of the simulated ratios are as large, or larger than, the observed ratio of 2.017. Because the observed ratio is unlikely to occur by random sampling alone when the population standard deviations are equal, we have fairly strong evidence that the generic blades (G) really do have more variability in the number of shaves than the name brand blades (B). Question 6 (continued) Scoring This question is scored in four sections. Section 1 consists of parts (a) and (b); section 2 consists of part (c); section 3 consists of part (d); and section 4 consists of part (e). Sections 1, 2, 3, and 4 are scored as essentially correct (E), partially correct (P), or incorrect (I). Section 1 is scored as follows: Essentially correct (E) if the response constructs the back-to-back stemplot correctly AND correctly compares the two distributions on all three characteristics (center, spread, shape). Partially correct (P) if the response: Correctly constructs the graph AND Correctly compares one or two of the three characteristics; OR Discusses all three characteristics without comparison. OR Incorrectly constructs the graph AND correctly compares all three characteristics. Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P. Section 2 is scored as follows: Essentially correct (E) if the response provides a correct conclusion in context with justification based on linkage to the p-value. Partially correct (P) if the response provides a correct conclusion in context, but no justification based on linkage to the p-value; OR if the response provides a correct conclusion with justification based on linkage to the p-value, but no context. Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P. Notes: If the correct conclusion for the reported p-value is to fail to reject the null hypothesis, then a conclusion that is equivalent to accepting the null hypothesis, either as a stated decision or as a conclusion in context, cannot be scored as E (for example, "Name brand blades last the same length of time as the generic blades.") Such a response is scored P provided that the conclusion is in context with linkage. If such a response lacks either context or linkage, it is scored I. If the correct conclusion for the reported p-value is to fail to reject, but the response says to "retain the null hypothesis" with linkage and context, the response is scored P. However, if the response goes on to say something equivalent to "fail to reject" in context, the response is scored E. If both a significance level $\alpha$ and a p-value are given together, the linkage between the p-value and the conclusion is implied. If no significance level is given, the solution must be explicit about the linkage by giving a correct interpretation of the p-value or explaining how the conclusion follows from the p-value, such as "Because the p-value is large, we do not reject the null hypothesis." - o o - - - - Question 6 (continued) Section 3 is scored as follows: Essentially correct (E) if the response correctly states that the population standard deviations are equal if the null hypothesis is true, so the (simulated) sample standard deviations should produce ratios centered around 1. Partially correct (P) if the response does not appeal to the equality of the population standard deviations under the null hypothesis but makes the connection to the simulated ratios being centered around 1; OR if the response appeals to the equality of the population standard deviations under the null hypothesis, but does not make the connection that the simulated sample standard deviations should produce ratios centered around 1. Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P. Section 4 is scored as follows: Essentially correct (E) if the response draws an appropriate conclusion with a well-explained justification based on the results of the simulation. Partially correct (P) if the response draws an appropriate conclusion based on the results of the simulation, but with a weak or poorly communicated justification. Incorrect (I) if the response does not meet the criteria for E or P. Each essentially correct (E) section counts as 1 point. Each partially correct (P) section counts as 1/2 point. 4 Complete Response 3 Substantial Response 2 Developing Response 1 Minimal Response If a response is between two scores (for example, 21/2 points), use a holistic approach to decide whether to score up or down, depending on the overall strength of the response and communication.

prompt:
Natural gas is used in some households to heat the home, to heat the water, and to cook. A utility company sent the following bar chart to a household to show the amount of natural gas, measured in therms, that the household used last year. The chart shows the number of therms and the average monthly temperature, in degrees Fahrenheit, for each month of the year.

(a) Describe how the number of therms used each month changed over the year.

(b) Construct an appropriate graph that shows the relationship between the number of therms used and the average monthly temperature.

(c) Describe what your graph in part (b) reveals about the relationship between the number of therms used and the average monthly temperature that is not revealed on the bar chart sent by the utility company.

points.criteria: Part (a) is essentially correct if the response notes the decreasing and then increasing trend in therms used, identifies July as the low point, and describes the trend in context. Part (b) is essentially correct if the response gives a scatterplot of average monthly temperature and therms, with all points in approximately correct locations and correctly placed scales and labels. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response describes the relationship as moderately strong, negative, curved or approximately linear, and uses the context of therms and temperature. A complete response has three essentially correct parts; substantial/developing/minimal scores follow the official E/P/I section count.

prompt:
Swedish researchers investigated the relationship between chocolate consumption and stroke. The researchers gave a questionnaire about eating habits to a randomly selected sample of Swedish men. Based on the responses, the men were classified into two groups. Group A consisted of the 9,250 men who ate the most chocolate per week, and group B consisted of the 9,250 men who ate the least chocolate per week. The researchers tracked the men's health for ten years. During that time, there were 458 cases of stroke among group A and 543 cases of stroke among group B.

(a) Do the data provide convincing statistical evidence that Swedish men who would be classified into group A have a lower probability of stroke than Swedish men who would be classified into group B?

(b) A report in a newspaper concluded that Swedish men can reduce their probability of stroke by eating more chocolate. Based on the description of the investigation, was the conclusion appropriate? Justify your answer.

points.criteria: Part (a) is scored in sections for hypotheses/test statistic and p-value, procedure and conditions, and conclusion. Correct work uses a two-sample z-test for proportions with hypotheses such as $H_0:p_A=p_B$ and $H_a:p_A<p_B$, checks random sampling and large-count conditions, and links a very small p-value to a conclusion in context. Part (b) is essentially correct if the response states that the newspaper's cause-and-effect conclusion is not justified because the investigation was observational rather than a randomized experiment. A complete response has four essentially correct sections; partial credit follows the official E/P/I section rules.

prompt:
A large retail company has 500 stores in the United States and 300 stores in Europe. The average number of employees per store is 200, for a total of 100,000 employees in the United States and 60,000 employees in Europe. The company is considering offering employees one of two new benefits: one additional day of paid vacation per year or a small increase in pay. A survey will be given to a sample of employees to investigate which benefit is preferred and whether there is a difference in preference between employees in the United States and employees in Europe.

Sampling method 1: randomly select 8 stores from the 800 stores; all employees at the selected stores are surveyed.

Sampling method 2: randomly select 1,000 employees from the United States stores and 600 employees from the European stores; all 1,600 selected employees are surveyed.

(a)(i) Identify the method that gives a stratified sample and identify the strata. (ii) Identify the method that gives a cluster sample and identify the clusters.

(b) Give one statistical advantage and one statistical disadvantage of using sampling method 1.

(c) Explain a statistical advantage of using sampling method 2 rather than using a simple random sample.

points.criteria: Part (a) is essentially correct if method 2 is identified as stratified sampling with strata US employees and European employees, and method 1 is identified as cluster sampling with stores as clusters. Part (b) requires a plausible statistical advantage of cluster sampling based on surveying employees located in only 8 stores, and a plausible disadvantage based on representativeness or within-store dependence. Part (c) requires a statistical advantage of stratified sampling over simple random sampling, such as guaranteeing representation from both regions in the same proportions as the population and reducing sampling variability. Scoring follows the official three-section E/P/I rubric.

prompt:
In ecology, a measure of diversity of a species in a certain population is called the diversity index. The diversity index $D$ is defined as the probability that any two organisms selected at random from the population will be from different species.

(a) If every organism in a population is from the same species, what is the value of $D$? Explain.

(b) Consider a large population of turtles in which 30 percent are snapping turtles and 70 percent are box turtles. What is the value of $D$ for the population?

(c) Suppose a large population of turtles consists only of snapping turtles and box turtles, but the percentages are not known. What is the maximum possible value of $D$? Justify your answer.

points.criteria: Part (a) is essentially correct if the response gives $D=0$ and explains that two organisms cannot be from different species when all organisms are the same species. Part (b) is essentially correct if the response shows the probability calculation $(0.3)(0.7)+(0.7)(0.3)=0.42$. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response identifies the maximum as 0.5 and justifies it using equal representation, the expression $2p(1-p)$, or an equivalent maximization argument. Scoring follows the official three-part E/P/I rubric.

prompt:
A city council will fund a proposal to create a new city park if it concludes that more than 60 percent of city residents support the proposal. A survey of 2,000 randomly selected city residents will be conducted. Let $X$ represent the number in the sample who support the proposal. Assume $X$ is binomial.

(a) Determine the mean and standard deviation of $X$, assuming that 60 percent of city residents support the proposal.

(b) A normal distribution can be used to approximate the binomial distribution of $X$. The shaded middle 0.997 of the area is bounded by $k_1$ and $k_2$. Use a normal approximation and your results from part (a) to determine $k_1$ and $k_2$.

(c) The survey was conducted, and 1,293 of the 2,000 city residents supported the proposal. Do your answers in part (b) and the survey results support funding the proposal? Justify your answer.

points.criteria: Part (a) is essentially correct if the response gives mean $np=1200$, standard deviation $\sqrt{np(1-p)}=\sqrt{480}\approx21.91$, and shows the standard deviation calculation. Part (b) is essentially correct if the response gives endpoints around $1134$ to $1135$ and $1265$ to $1266$, justified by using approximately three standard deviations from the mean. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response concludes there is convincing evidence that more than 60 percent support the park, because 1,293 is above the entire interval from part (b). Scoring follows the official three-part E/P/I rubric.

prompt:
Phone callers to a bank's customer service center must wait until a service representative is available. The bank manager is interested in estimating the mean customer wait time. Thirteen calls were selected at random. A histogram of the 13 wait times, in minutes, is shown below.

(a) Based on the histogram, explain why it might not be appropriate to use a one-sample $t$-interval to estimate the mean wait time for all customers.

A logarithmic transformation is often used for data such as wait times. Let $x$ represent a customer's wait time. The table below shows the original wait times, the $\log_{10}(x)$ values, and summary statistics.

| Summary | Wait Time, $x$ | $\log_{10}(x)$ |
|---|---:|---:|
| Mean | 1.31 | 0.0632 |
| Median | 1.20 | 0.0792 |
| Standard deviation | 0.679 | 0.235 |

(b) Based on the histogram, the conditions for inference have been met for the log-transformed data. Construct and interpret a 95 percent confidence interval for the population mean $\mu$ of the log of the wait times.

(c) Convert the endpoints of your interval in part (b) back to minutes and write the resulting interval.

(d) Consider the parameter $10^\mu$ in the population distribution of wait times. (i) How does $10^\mu$ compare with the median? (ii) How does $10^\mu$ compare with the mean?

(e) Write an interpretation of the interval you constructed in part (c).

points.criteria: Section 1, part (a), is essentially correct if the response states that the wait-time distribution is skewed or not symmetric and that the sample size is small. Section 2, parts (b) and (c), is essentially correct if the response reports the interval $0.063\pm2.179(0.235/\sqrt{13})=(-0.079,0.205)$, interprets it as a confidence interval for the mean of the log wait times, and converts endpoints to approximately 0.834 and 1.604 minutes. Section 3, part (d), is essentially correct if the response states that $10^\mu$ equals the population median wait time and is less than the population mean wait time. Section 4, part (e), is essentially correct if the response interprets the interval from part (c) as a 95 percent confidence interval for the population median wait time. Scoring follows the official four-section E/P/I rubric.

Population pyramids are a type of bar chart that show the distribution of ages of a country's population. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response identifies 30 to 39 as the median male age group for Country A and supports the choice using cumulative proportions or an equivalent explanation. prompt:
Researchers conducted a study to investigate whether playing video games could improve a person's ability to visually search. Three video games were used: a driving game, a sports game, and a puzzle game. The participants consisted of 60 volunteers with no previous experience playing video games. Each participant was assigned to play one of the three games for one hour each day for one month. At the beginning and end of the month, the participants completed a visual-search task, and the improvement time was recorded.

(a) Describe a method for randomly assigning the 60 volunteers to three groups of 20, one group for each video game.

(b) The researchers want to compare the mean improvement times for the driving game and sports game groups. Identify the appropriate test procedure and state appropriate hypotheses.

(c) Describe one way the researchers could increase the power of the test and explain why it would increase power.

points.criteria: Part (a) requires a random assignment method that uses a random process, gives equal probability of assignment, produces 20 volunteers per group, and specifies which game is assigned to each group. Part (b) requires a two-sample t-test for a difference in means, parameters in context, and hypotheses such as $H_0:\mu_D=\mu_S$ and $H_a:\mu_D\ne\mu_S$. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response identifies increasing sample size or increasing alpha and gives a statistical explanation related to power. The official scoring uses E/P/I scores for parts (a), (b), and (c).

At the beginning and end of the month, the participants completed a visual-search task, and the improvement time was recorded.

(a) Describe a method for randomly assigning the 60 volunteers to three groups of 20, one group for each video game.

(b) The researchers want to compare the mean improvement times for the driving game and sports game groups. Identify the appropriate test procedure and state appropriate hypotheses.

(c) Describe one way the researchers could increase the power of the test and explain why it would increase power.
rubric_text: Part (a) requires a random assignment method that uses a random process, gives equal probability of assignment, produces 20 volunteers per group, and specifies which game is assigned to each group. Part (b) requires a two-sample t-test for a difference in means, parameters in context, and hypotheses such as $H_0:\mu_D=\mu_S$ and $H_a:\mu_D\ne\mu_S$. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response identifies increasing sample size or increasing alpha and gives a statistical explanation related to power. The official scoring uses E/P/I scores for parts (a), (b), and (c).

prompt:
In women's tennis, a player must win 2 out of 3 sets to win a match. If a player wins the first 2 sets, she wins the match and the third set is not played. Player V and Player M will compete in a match.

(a) Let V represent the event that Player V wins a set, and let M represent the event that Player M wins a set. (i) List all possible sequences of events V and M by set played that will result in Player V winning the match. (ii) List all possible sequences that will result in Player M winning the match.

(b) Suppose that for the first set $P(V)=0.5$, for the second set $P(V)=0.6$ if V won the first set and $P(V)=0.3$ if M won the first set, and for the third set $P(V)=0.45$ regardless of the first two sets. Find the probability that Player V wins the match.

(c) Given that Player V wins the match, find the probability that the match lasts 3 sets.

(d) Find the expected number of sets played in the match.

points.criteria: Section 1 requires all correct match-winning sequences for V and M, with no third set after one player wins the first two sets. Section 2 requires the correct probability that V wins, 0.4575, linked to the listed outcomes, and the correct conditional probability for a three-set V win, approximately 0.344. Section 3 requires the probability distribution for the number of sets played and expected value 2.35 sets. The official scoring uses three E/P/I sections.

prompt:
A sociologist was investigating the ages of grandparents of high school students. From a random sample of 10 high school students, the sociologist collected data on the current ages, in years, of the students' maternal grandparents.

| Student | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I | J | Mean | Standard Deviation |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Age of grandmother | 75 | 70 | 55 | 75 | 55 | 70 | 80 | 70 | 74 | 74 | 69.8 | 8.38 |
| Age of grandfather | 74 | 75 | 65 | 67 | 60 | 78 | 83 | 74 | 70 | 70 | 71.6 | 6.65 |
| Difference | 1 | -5 | -10 | 8 | -5 | -8 | -3 | -4 | 4 | 4 | -1.8 | 5.81 |

(a) Construct and interpret a 95 percent confidence interval for the population mean difference in ages of grandparents, defined as grandmother's age minus grandfather's age.

(b) Without taking into account which grandparent is older, use the data to estimate the mean of the age differences for all maternal grandparents of high school students.

points.criteria: Section 1 requires identifying a paired t-interval for a population mean difference and checking random sample and normality conditions using the differences. Section 2 requires the correct interval, approximately -5.96 to 2.36 years, using 9 degrees of freedom. Section 3 requires a contextual 95 percent confidence interpretation for the population mean difference in grandparents' ages. Section 4 requires a point estimate using the mean of the absolute values of the differences, 5.2 years, with supporting work. The official scoring uses four E/P/I sections.

prompt:
An automobile manufacturer sold 30,000 new cars, one to each of 30,000 customers, in a certain year. The manufacturer was interested in investigating the proportion of the new cars that experienced a mechanical problem within the first 5,000 miles driven.

(a) A list of the names and addresses of all 30,000 customers was available. Describe a method for obtaining a simple random sample of 1,000 customers from the 30,000 customers.

Each customer from a simple random sample of 1,000 customers was asked whether they experienced any mechanical problems within the first 5,000 miles driven. Forty customers reported at least one problem. Of those 40 customers, 13 reported a problem with power door locks.

(b) Explain why 32.5 percent should not be used to estimate the proportion of all 30,000 new cars that had power door lock problems.

(c) Use the sample results to give a point estimate for the number of the 30,000 new cars that had power door lock problems.

points.criteria: Part (a) is essentially correct if the sampling plan uses a random process, gives a sample size of 1,000, makes every possible sample equally likely, and specifies that customers are sampled. Part (b) is essentially correct if the response explains that 32.5 percent is conditional on the subset with any problem, not the entire sample or population. Part (c) is essentially correct if the response estimates 390 cars, using 13 out of 1,000 or 1.3 percent of 30,000. The official scoring uses E/P/I scores for the three parts.

prompt:
Emily walks every day and records the number of miles she walks each day. The histogram and five-number summary below were created from the recorded miles for a random sample of 25 days.

(a) One method for identifying an outlier is to use the interquartile range. An outlier is any number greater than the upper quartile by at least 1.5 times the IQR or less than the lower quartile by at least 1.5 times the IQR. Does 7.5 miles appear to be an outlier? Justify your answer.

Another method of identifying an outlier is to investigate whether there is evidence that a value might have come from a population with a mean different from the mean of the population of the other values. Let $X$ and $Y$ represent random variables. $X$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu_X$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, and $Y$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu_Y$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. Consider the difference $Y-\bar{X}$, where $Y$ is a randomly selected value of $Y$ and $\bar{X}$ is the mean of $n-1$ randomly selected values of $X$.

(b) In terms of $\mu_X$ and $\mu_Y$, what is the mean of $Y-\bar{X}$? In terms of $n$ and $\sigma$, what is the standard deviation of $Y-\bar{X}$?

Suppose $n=25$, the value 7.5 is used as $Y$, and the remaining 24 values have $\bar{x}=3.171$ and $s=0.821$.

(c) Use the value of the potential outlier and the summary statistics of the remaining 24 values to estimate the mean and standard deviation of $Y-\bar{X}$.

(d) Calculate the test statistic.

(e) The p-value for the test statistic in part (d) is approximately 0. Explain what this p-value means and whether 7.5 is an outlier by this method.

points.criteria: Section 1 requires computing the upper outlier cutoff $Q_3+1.5(Q_3-Q_1)=5.6$, comparing 7.5 to the cutoff, and concluding consistently. Section 2 requires the formulas $E(Y-\bar{X})=\mu_Y-\mu_X$ and $SD(Y-\bar{X})=\sigma\sqrt{1+1/(n-1)}$, plus estimates 4.329 and about 0.838. Section 3 requires using the estimates from part (c) to calculate a test statistic of about 5.17. Section 4 requires linking the very small p-value to evidence that the population means differ and concluding that 7.5 is an outlier by the new method. The official scoring uses four E/P/I sections.
